skill.name: mac-automation
skill.description: Automate macOS apps and system actions with AppleScript examples for Mail, Calendar, Reminders, Safari, Finder, and system controls.
skill.summary: Run guided AppleScript automation for common macOS apps and local system tasks.
skill.author: 7Sageer, Claude

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified extensive external command usage, but this is the declared purpose of the skill: guiding osascript and AppleScript automation. I found no evidence of prompt injection, hidden network exfiltration, malware staging, or confirmed malicious intent. The skill should publish with a warning because it can read private local data, manipulate files, send mail, run shell commands through AppleScript, and perform disruptive system actions.

medium_findings.title: Broad Local Command Execution Through AppleScript
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill teaches agents to run osascript from Bash and includes AppleScript do shell script examples. This is legitimate for a macOS automation skill, but it can execute local commands and inherits the user permissions granted to the agent and macOS Automation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution pattern is explicit and central to the skill. The surrounding context shows intended local automation rather than hidden execution, so the risk is real but dual-use.

medium_findings.title: Access to Private Local Data
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The references include reading unread Mail metadata, Calendar data, clipboard contents, Safari page source, and selected page text. This can expose sensitive personal or business information if used without narrow user consent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples directly retrieve local application data and browser content. The skill also instructs privacy-sensitive data to be displayed only when requested, which reduces malicious intent but not exposure risk.

medium_findings.title: Destructive and Disruptive Automation Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill includes examples for deleting Mail, reminders, calendar events, calendar lists, files, emptying Trash, and power actions such as restart or shutdown. These are expected automation capabilities but require confirmation and clear user intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples perform concrete destructive or disruptive actions. The skill documents confirmation for destructive operations, so this is a controlled dual-use risk rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Screen Capture and Keyboard Simulation Capabilities
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: Static keylogger and screen capture upload alerts are overstated because the files show screenshot creation and keystroke simulation, not key capture or upload. These capabilities are still privacy-sensitive and can affect active applications.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain screenshot and keystroke commands, but no evidence of uploading screenshots or recording keystrokes. The remaining risk is legitimate automation that can still be misused.

medium_findings.title: Safari JavaScript Execution and Form Interaction
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Safari references execute JavaScript in the active tab, click page elements, and fill form fields. This can automate useful browser tasks, but it can also alter web pages or interact with authenticated sessions if misused.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The JavaScript execution and form manipulation examples are explicit. No malicious target or credential exfiltration is shown, so this is a medium dual-use browser automation concern.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Benign Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The Safari URLs point to common example destinations such as Google, Apple, GitHub, and example.com. I found no evidence that these URLs receive local files, secrets, screenshots, or private application data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible sample navigation targets and search examples. No upload, webhook, credential, or suspicious domain evidence was found in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported weak cryptography locations are ordinary AppleScript or documentation text, not MD5, SHA1, or cryptographic code. These alerts appear to be pattern matches against unrelated words or Markdown structure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations contain frontmatter, event creation examples, and tips, with no cryptographic algorithm usage. This strongly supports a false-positive classification.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE_CHECK: I checked for text that tries to override evaluator instructions, claim pre-approval, or skip analysis. No evidence found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted searches did not find injection phrases. Confidence is high enough for this audit, but it is based on reviewed files and targeted checks rather than exhaustive semantic proof.

dangerous_patterns.title: osascript Invocation from Bash
dangerous_patterns.description: The core pattern runs AppleScript through the shell. This is necessary for the skill but should require explicit user intent for each action.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The invocation pattern is directly documented as the core principle of the skill. Its risk depends on the generated AppleScript and user permissions.

dangerous_patterns.title: AppleScript Shell Command Bridge
dangerous_patterns.description: Some examples use AppleScript do shell script to run local shell utilities such as vm_stat, screencapture, caffeinate, pmset, networksetup, and defaults.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and locally scoped. They are not hidden, but they expand the automation surface beyond application scripting.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Screenshot Capture
dangerous_patterns.description: The system reference shows saving screenshots to the Desktop. There is no upload step, but screenshots can contain sensitive information.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The screenshot behavior is directly shown, and the absence of network transfer lowers severity. Privacy risk remains because local screen contents may be sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Finder and Application Data Mutation
dangerous_patterns.description: The references include file movement, deletion, Trash emptying, Mail deletion, and calendar deletion operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The mutation and deletion examples are concrete. The skill warns about destructive operations, but the commands can still cause data loss if misused.

dangerous_patterns.title: Safari DOM Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: The Safari reference runs JavaScript in the active tab and can read page text, page source, links, and form fields.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The DOM access is directly documented and can touch authenticated browser context. No malicious destination is shown, so the concern is capability-based.

content.value_statement: Manual macOS tasks can be slow when they span Mail, Calendar, Finder, Safari, and system settings. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured AppleScript patterns for local automation.
actual_capabilities: Create, read, search, and delete Mail messages using AppleScript examples.
Create and manage Reminders and Calendar events with due dates and details.
Open Safari URLs, inspect page details, manage tabs, and run page JavaScript.
Move, copy, rename, delete, and reveal files through Finder automation patterns.
Control local macOS functions such as notifications, volume, clipboard, apps, screenshots, and power actions.
Provide troubleshooting guidance for macOS Automation and Accessibility permissions.
limitations: Requires macOS with AppleScript support and local permission prompts approved by the user.
Examples may need app names, paths, calendars, or lists changed for each system.
Some Safari JavaScript actions require the Safari developer setting for JavaScript from Apple Events.
Destructive commands can cause data loss if used without confirmation.

use_cases.title: Personal productivity shortcuts
use_cases.description: Create reminders, calendar events, notifications, and clipboard-based notes without switching between several apps.

use_cases.title: Office workflow automation
use_cases.description: Draft or send Mail messages, review unread mail summaries, and organize files with repeatable local automation.

use_cases.title: Browser and file task scripting
use_cases.description: Open Safari pages, inspect tab information, run controlled JavaScript, and manage Finder files during support workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a reminder
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner task for a simple local action.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mac-automation skill to create a reminder for [task] due [date and time]. Show me the AppleScript before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize today
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate task that reads local app data.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use AppleScript to get today's Calendar events and unread Mail subjects. Only show the sender, subject, and event title.

prompt_templates.title: Organize files
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a Finder automation plan to move [file type] files from [source folder] to [target folder]. Ask before moving anything.

prompt_templates.title: Automate Safari workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced task involving browser state and JavaScript.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Safari AppleScript to open [site], collect the current page title and selected text, then propose the next automation step before acting.

output_examples.input: Create a reminder from my clipboard for tomorrow morning.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks clipboard content, creates a Reminders item for tomorrow at 9:00, and confirms the reminder title.

output_examples.input: Show my unread email count and today's meetings.
output: Unread mail count with recent sender and subject details.
Today's calendar events with times and titles.
A note if permissions are missing.

output_examples.input: Open GitHub in Safari and get the current page title.
output_examples.output: Safari opens the requested page, returns the active tab title, and reports any permission or window errors.

best_practices: Ask for confirmation before sending mail, deleting data, changing files, or triggering power actions.
Show the planned AppleScript for sensitive actions before execution.
Limit output to the fields the user requested when reading private app data.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated AppleScript that includes unreviewed shell commands or user-provided paths.
Do not read Mail, clipboard, browser content, or calendar data without explicit user request.
Do not empty Trash, delete app data, send email, or shut down the Mac as part of a broad request.

faq.question: Does this skill run code on my Mac?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides the assistant to run AppleScript through osascript, so user approval and local permissions matter.

faq.question: Why do macOS permission prompts appear?
faq.answer: macOS requires Automation, Accessibility, and app permissions before one app can control another app.

faq.question: Can it read private information?
faq.answer: Yes, if requested. Examples can read Mail metadata, Calendar events, clipboard text, Safari page data, and Finder details.

faq.question: Can it send emails or delete files?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for sending Mail and deleting data, so those actions should require confirmation.

faq.question: Does it contact external servers?
faq.answer: It includes Safari navigation examples and Google search URLs. I found no evidence of hidden data upload or exfiltration.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
